metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Earth Sciences 11 is a senior course examining the physical systems that shape our planet — from minerals and rocks to plate tectonics, atmosphere, ocean, and the solar system. Students investigate geological, meteorological, hydrological, and astronomical phenomena through inquiry, data analysis, and field-based perspectives. First Peoples knowledge of geological phenomena, weather, and the land is integrated throughout as a valid and complementary scientific perspective.
metadata.general_description: A senior Earth science course covering earth materials, plate tectonics, atmospheric and climate systems, water distribution, and astronomy — developing scientific inquiry, data analysis, and environmental literacy.

big_ideas.statement: Earth materials are changed as they cycle through the geosphere and are used as resources, with economic and environmental implications.
big_ideas.elaboration: Minerals and rocks form, transform, and are recycled through the rock cycle over geological time. Human extraction of these materials for economic use has significant environmental and social consequences.

big_ideas.statement: Plate tectonic theory explains the consequences of tectonic plate interactions.
big_ideas.elaboration: The movement of tectonic plates drives earthquakes, volcanism, mountain building, and ocean basin formation. This unifying theory connects geological phenomena across scales, from local BC geology to global continental distribution.

big_ideas.statement: The transfer of energy through the atmosphere creates weather, and this transfer is affected by climate change.
big_ideas.elaboration: Solar radiation drives atmospheric circulation, creating weather patterns. Human-caused changes to atmospheric composition are altering these energy flows, leading to climate change with cascading local and global effects.

big_ideas.statement: The distribution of water has a major influence on weather and climate.
big_ideas.elaboration: Oceans and freshwater systems regulate Earth's temperature, drive weather systems, and support life. Their distribution and properties — salinity, currents, thermal capacity — are central to understanding Earth's climate system.

big_ideas.statement: Astronomy seeks to explain the origin and interactions of Earth and its solar system.
big_ideas.elaboration: Earth exists within a solar system that formed from a nebula 4.6 billion years ago. Understanding Earth's place in space — its relationship with the sun and moon, stellar classification, and the nebular hypothesis — provides essential context for understanding Earth processes.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity; formulate hypotheses about natural phenomena; make observations to identify questions.
i_can_statements: I can identify complex questions about geological, meteorological, hydrological, and astronomical phenomena.
I can make careful field and laboratory observations and generate testable hypotheses.
I can predict multiple possible outcomes based on Earth science principles.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively design investigations using appropriate methods and equipment; collect reliable data; apply accuracy and precision concepts.
i_can_statements: I can plan Earth science investigations — including fieldwork — selecting appropriate methods and tools.
I can collect accurate field and laboratory data using appropriate SI units and instruments.
I can apply accuracy and precision concepts when measuring geological and atmospheric quantities.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Identify patterns in data; apply scientific concepts to draw evidence-based conclusions; apply First Peoples perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples knowledge of land, water, and sky to enrich my scientific analysis.
I can identify patterns in geological, meteorological, and hydrological data sets.
I can construct and interpret diagrams including rock cycle, cross-sections, climate graphs, and star charts.
I can draw evidence-based conclusions about Earth processes from multiple lines of data.

competencies.raw: Assess methods; validate models; recognize bias; consider social and ethical implications of resource use and climate change.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the methods of an Earth science investigation and identify sources of uncertainty.
I can assess the validity of models such as plate tectonics or climate projections.
I can consider the economic, environmental, and ethical implications of resource extraction.
I can analyze evidence for climate change with informed skepticism and evaluate competing claims.

competencies.raw: Design solutions to problems; contribute to care for community and environment; consider scientists' roles in addressing Earth's challenges.
i_can_statements: I can apply Earth science concepts to address real-world problems such as resource management or natural disaster preparedness.
I can consider scientists' roles in environmental stewardship and sustainable resource use.
I can connect Earth science to local BC geology, climate, and First Peoples land stewardship.

competencies.raw: Construct evidence-based arguments using appropriate scientific language and representations.
i_can_statements: I can communicate Earth science concepts using correct terminology, maps, and diagrams.
I can write reports that present evidence-based arguments about geological, atmospheric, or astronomical phenomena.
I can represent Earth processes using models, cross-sections, and data visualizations.

areas.name: Earth Materials and the Rock Cycle
areas.raw: Earth materials are changed as they cycle through the geosphere and are used as resources.
topics: Minerals: definition, identification properties (hardness, cleavage, lustre, colour), common minerals
Igneous rocks: intrusive vs. extrusive, formation, texture, and examples
Sedimentary rocks: clastic, chemical, and organic; formation and examples
Metamorphic rocks: contact and regional metamorphism; foliated and non-foliated
The rock cycle: processes connecting igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks
Geological resources: mining, fossil fuels, and industrial minerals in BC
Economic and environmental implications of resource extraction

areas.name: Plate Tectonics
areas.raw: Plate tectonic theory explains the consequences of tectonic plate interactions.
topics: Evidence for plate tectonics: seafloor spreading, paleomagnetism, fossil distribution, continental fit
Plate motion: convection currents, divergent, convergent, and transform boundaries
Consequences of plate interactions: earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain building, ocean trenches
BC's tectonic setting: the Cascadia Subduction Zone, Juan de Fuca Plate, and earthquake risk
First Peoples knowledge and oral histories of geological events and landforms

areas.name: Atmosphere, Weather, and Climate
areas.raw: The transfer of energy through the atmosphere creates weather, and this transfer is affected by climate change.
topics: Atmospheric composition and layers: troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere
The hydrologic cycle: evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation, runoff
Solar radiation and Earth's energy budget: albedo, greenhouse effect, and energy balance
Weather systems: air masses, fronts, high and low pressure systems
Extreme weather events: hurricanes, tornadoes, blizzards, and their formation
Climate vs. weather: long-term patterns and variability
Climate change: evidence, causes (CO₂, methane, deforestation), and projected impacts
First Peoples perspectives on weather patterns and climate knowledge

areas.name: Water Systems
areas.raw: The distribution of water has a major influence on weather and climate.
topics: Global water distribution: oceans, glaciers, groundwater, freshwater
Ocean properties: salinity, temperature, density, and their effects on climate
Ocean currents: surface currents (wind-driven) and thermohaline circulation (deep ocean)
Ocean influence on climate: heat storage, BC's marine climate
Freshwater systems: rivers, lakes, wetlands, glaciers, and groundwater
Climate change effects on water: sea level rise, glacial retreat, changing precipitation

areas.name: Astronomy
areas.raw: Astronomy seeks to explain the origin and interactions of Earth and its solar system.
topics: Nebular hypothesis: formation of the solar system from a solar nebula
Earth's unique properties: distance from sun, atmosphere, liquid water, magnetic field
Earth-moon-sun system: tides, eclipses, lunar phases, seasons
Stellar classification: colour, temperature, luminosity, and the H-R diagram
Life cycle of stars: main sequence, red giant, white dwarf, neutron star, black hole
Space technologies: telescopes, satellites, space probes, and their scientific contributions
